games.explanation: The market is pricing this like the Yankees just have to show up, and the numbers underneath say Will Warren is exactly the kind of arm who makes that assumption expensive. Warren has been fine against righties and genuinely leaky against lefties, which is a problem when Brandon Lowe and Bryan Reynolds are waiting in a park that plays neutral for runs but juices the ball out. Bubba Chandler is the quieter half of this equation, sitting on a sub-.300 xwOBA in his small-sample look and giving Pittsburgh a real chance to keep pace with a Yankees lineup that isn't quite the wrecking crew the price implies. At +130 on a game the sims call a coin flip, you're getting paid to take the better Elo team.

The market is pricing this like a coin flip with a lean, but the gap between these two rosters is wider than Baltimore's ballpark and Boston is the sturdier operation on both sides of the ball. Bradish's numbers look shiny in a tiny sample, and the one soft spot that keeps showing up is left-handed hitters, which happens to be the entire shape of the Red Sox lineup with Abreu and the switch-hitters batting from that side. Suarez is the calmer bet on the mound, a strike-thrower who avoids the barrel and doesn't hand out the free passes that keep Alonso and Henderson in business. At -136 you're laying a reasonable number on the better team facing the pitcher whose weakness lines up with your lineup's strength. That's the whole pitch.

Jack Perkins is a strike-throwing project the market is treating like an actual big league starter, and Arizona has the exact lineup built to expose that. A near-13% walk rate against a Marte-Perdomo-Carroll top four is the kind of arithmetic that ends in a four-run inning before anyone throws a barrel. The Diamondbacks are the better team by a wide margin here, facing a rookie who's issued free passes to roughly one in every eight hitters he's seen. At -134, you're paying fair freight to be on the side with the pitcher who can find the zone and the hitters who punish anyone who can't.

Chase Burns has a strikeout rate that makes him look like a problem until you notice the platoon split does most of the heavy lifting. Seattle happens to be built to exploit exactly what he doesn't do well, rolling out a lineup where the lefty bats have been tagging right-handed pitching all year and Burns has coughed up a .323 xwOBA to that side of the plate. Castillo isn't the ace he used to be, but a closed roof in a park that suppresses runs is a friendly enough backdrop, and the sim landing on a near dead-even 3.6 to 3.8 tells you this is a coin flip the market has miscounted. At -110 on the better team in a low-scoring game, you're getting a fair price on the wrong favorite.

At -136 you're laying a reasonable number on the better team facing the pitcher whose weakness lines up with your lineup's strength. That's the whole pitch.

Milwaukee is the better team, nobody's arguing that, but a run and a half is a lot of cushion to hand out in a game the projection sees decided by a single run. Brandon Sproat has walked nearly 11% of the hitters he's faced in a very short big league sample, and a Soto-led lineup that hammers righties is exactly the wrong audience for a command-shaky rookie. The Brewers' top three are solid without being scary, so the path to a blowout requires Milwaukee's bullpen to slam the door on a lineup that's been posting a .463 xwOBA against right-handers at the top. Getting a plus-money number to a runner-up finish is the whole ballgame here, and the market is paying you to bet the game stays close.
